title: War arises between Constantine and Licinius on Account of the Christians.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: By this course he drew upon himself the emperor Constantine’s heaviest displeasure; and they became enemies, the pretended treaty of friendship between them having been violated.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Not long afterwards they took up arms against each other as declared enemies.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And after several engagements both by sea and land, Licinius was at last utterly defeated near Chrysopolis in Bithynia, a port of the Chalcedonians, and surrendered himself to Constantine.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Accordingly he having taken him alive, treated him with the utmost humanity, and would by no means put him to death, but ordered him to take up his abode and live in tranquillity at Thessalonica.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He having, however, remained quiet a short time, managed afterwards to collect some barbarian mercenaries and made an effort to repair his late disaster by a fresh appeal to arms.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The emperor being made acquainted with his proceedings, directed that he should be slain, which was carried into effect.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Constantine thus became possessed of the sole dominion, and was accordingly proclaimed sovereign Autocrat, and again sought to promote the welfare of Christians.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This he did in a variety of ways, and Christianity enjoyed unbroken peace by reason of his efforts.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But an internal dissension soon succeeded this state of repose, the nature and origin of which I shall now endeavor to describe.
